query: evidence on treatment of submandibular sialolithiasis

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The most common indication for submandibular gland excision is salivary stones with secondary ductal obstruction and sialadenitis. Transoral stone removal may be successful in cases of an isolated calculus within the submandibular duct (see Image . Submandibular Gland Calculi). Sialendoscopy is a widely used minimally invasive treatment that can be both diagnostic and therapeutic, particularly when stones are smaller than 7 mm in diameter (see Image . Endoscopy View from Sialendoscopy). [15] However, most patients with salivary calculi experience recurrent obstruction and sialadenitis that do not resolve with minimally invasive measures alone, often necessitating submandibular gland excision.

retrieved_docs.title: Sialolithiasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of sialolithiasis. Review the evaluation of sialolithiasis. Outline the treatment and management options available for sialolithiasis. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to improve clinical care and outcomes for patients with sialolithiasis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess patients with submandibular gland disease for indications and contraindications to submandibular gland excision. Determine the most suitable approach to submandibular gland excision based on pathology, patient factors, and surgical considerations. Implement evidence-based, personalized strategies for managing common and rare complications of submandibular gland excision. Collaborate with all members of the interprofessional team, including primary and emergency care providers, oncologists, otolaryngologists, anesthesiologists, and nursing staff, to provide efficient, comprehensive, and coordinated care for individuals with submandibular gland disease. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Sialadenitis and Sialadenosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic sialadenitis: Medical management includes hydration, oral hygiene, pain relief, and sialogogues. In cases of infection, broad-spectrum antibiotics are added. In the case of sialolithiasis, salivary gland stone removal should occur using interventional sialendoscopy or direct surgical removal. [10] [11] Extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy, under ultrasonic guidance, is used for intraglandular duct stone removal.Recurrent sialadenitis (>3 episodes/year) or in chronic sclerosing sialadenitis: excision of the salivary gland is the recommendation.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Sialadenitis and Sialadenosis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Submandibular sialadenitis and sialadenosis are common causes of submandibular swelling. Patients with acute sialadenitis usually present in outpatient primary care or dental care and sometimes in an emergency care setting. Chronic sialadenitis evaluation can involve interventional radiologists, otolaryngologists, rheumatologists, and internists. Communication between the healthcare team is essential for proper treatment and management, especially in chronic sialadenitis and sialolithiasis, a multidisciplinary approach helps to discern the cause of this condition and manage sialadenosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Sialolithiasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Sialolithiasis is the most frequent cause of salivary gland swelling, affecting the major salivary glands: parotid, submandibular, and sublingual glands. The condition predominantly affects individuals between 30 and 60 years and is more common in men. The most frequent symptoms are cyclical gland swelling and pain associated with meals. Initial management is conservative, with various minimally invasive surgical options available for persistent cases. This activity reviews the presentation, evaluation, and treatment of sialolithiasis and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Sialolithiasis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: MR sialography demonstrates similar sensitivity and specificity to NCCT for the evaluation of sialolithiasis. Benefits of MR include excellent visualization of the salivary ducts without the need for contrast and no radiation exposure. Disadvantages include cost, image acquisition time, and limited availability compared to NCCT and Ultrasonography. [3] [16] Sialendoscopy allows for direct visualization of salivary stones and the salivary ducts, thus providing excellent sensitivity and specificity. In addition to aiding in the diagnosis of sialolithiasis, clinicians increasingly use sialoendoscopy for therapy and stone removal, given the advancements in endoscopic technology. [3] Research has shown sialoendoscopy to be a safe and effective alternative to conventional open surgical techniques with a more favorable complication profile. [18] Additionally, sialoendoscopy is performable in the outpatient setting utilizing local anesthesia.

retrieved_docs.title: Sialolithiasis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the case of larger salivary stones involving the distal submandibular duct (Wharton duct), diagnosis is often possible based on physical examination. Cases involving smaller stones within the distal submandibular duct or parotid duct (Stenson duct) were historically diagnosed utilizing conventional radiography, sialography, and digital subtraction sialography. Modern diagnostic techniques involve point-of-care ultrasound, computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and direct visualization with sialoendoscopy. [2] [3] A variety of treatment options exist for sialolithiasis, including sialogogues, direct massage of distal stones out of the duct, and other procedures including interventional sialography, lithotripsy, sialoendoscopy, and surgery. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Sialolithiasis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management of sialolithiasis should begin with conservative measures, including massaging the salivary gland, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, and sialogogues. Signs of infection, including cervical adenopathy, purulent discharge from the salivary ducts, or erythema surrounding the salivary ducts, indicate the need for antibiotic therapy. [4] [11] Further treatment is dictated based on the sialolith's size, number, and location if conservative management is unsuccessful. Mobile submandibular stones measuring less than 5 mm located within the distal duct should initially undergo management with endoscopy. Impacted submandibular stones within the distal duct and stones larger than 5 mm should have treatment with transoral duct slitting. Stones of 5 to 7 mm within the proximal duct or hilar region should receive initial treatment endoscopically. If this is unsuccessful or the stone becomes impacted, the next step is a transoral surgical approach.

retrieved_docs.title: Sialolithiasis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Sialolithiasis has an excellent prognosis, and the majority of patients can be managed conservatively with sialogogues and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. The minimally invasive procedures discussed above have excellent success rates with minimal morbidity compared to traditional surgical techniques. Sialadenectomy for the treatment of sialolithiasis is rarely necessary with modern treatment techniques. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding the principles of diagnosis and conservative management of sialadenitis and sialolithiasis, such as hydration, massage, sialogogues (lemon slices or sour candy), warm compresses, and antibiotics, is important for many clinicians and nurses, given the frequency of these conditions. Similarly, familiarity with the indications for surgery or specialist referral helps expedite and facilitate care for affected patients. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Sialadenitis and Sialadenosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Sialadenosis: This condition requires expectant management. Treatment of the underlying cause is the approach taken.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Parotid Gland -- Clinical Significance -- Sialolithiasis
retrieved_docs.content: Sialolithiasis is a benign condition caused when a stone or calculus is lodged in a salivary duct. This is the most common cause of obstructive salivary gland disease and is responsible for half of all major salivary gland disorders [10] Signs and symptoms include pain and swelling in the affected duct, particularly during and after eating [11] Ultrasound imaging is the first step in the diagnosis. Computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging, and magnetic resonance sialography can be used in patients with a high suspicion of ductal obstruction who had a negative or inconclusive ultrasound study [10] The goal of treatment is to increase saliva flow through the duct with oral hydration and drugs that induce salivary secretion. Surgical removal of the calculus is required for chronic sialolithiasis that has failed conservative treatment [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Sialolithiasis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Sialolithiasis is the most common benign cause of salivary gland swelling; however, it remains a relatively rare diagnosis with an incidence of 1 in 10,000 to 1 in 30,000. The disease commonly gets diagnosed in the primary care or emergency medicine setting, where initial patient contact is mostly with nurses, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, primary care physicians, and emergency medicine physicians. Given the oral nature of the disease, some patients may initially present to their dentist for evaluation of symptoms. Diagnostic radiologists are often involved in the initial workup for imaging recommendations and interpretation. The potential involvement of all these disciplines is why an interprofessional team approach is necessary for the diagnosis and management of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Submandibular Salivary Gland Excision -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: With an annual incidence of approximately 60 cases per 100,000 individuals, salivary gland pathologies are frequently encountered by primary and emergency care providers, as well as otolaryngologists and head-and-neck surgeons. [30] Up to 1% of the population may develop salivary calculi, which may or may not be symptomatic. Acute suppurative sialadenitis, although comparatively rare (0.02% of hospital admissions), carries a mortality rate of up to 40%. [31] [32] Recognition of these conditions is therefore critical for most clinicians.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the anatomic site associated with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer is classified under the broader category of thyroid disorders (اضطراب صماوي) and is clinically linked to the surgical removal of the thyroid gland. Surgical management of thyroid disease includes thyroidectomy, which may be performed as a complete removal (استئصال الدرقية) or as a partial removal (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي). Both complete and partial thyroidectomy are sub‑procedures encompassed by the general procedure termed “thyroidectomy; partial or complete.” The head and neck region is therefore the target anatomy for these thyroidectomy procedures. Additionally, partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with the anatomical reference “دراق,” reflecting its relationship to the thyroid gland itself. Understanding the connections among the head and neck anatomy, thyroid cancer, underlying thyroid disorders, and the spectrum of thyroidectomy techniques is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Excision is described as a broad surgical approach that encompasses a variety of operative techniques used to remove tissue, explore anatomical regions, or treat disease. Specific procedures classified under the Excision method include partial or complete adrenalectomy with or without adrenal gland biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal routes; cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, with or without choledochoenterostomy; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial locations; suboccipital craniectomy aimed at cranial‑nerve exploration or decompression; radical orchiectomy for tumor removal that incorporates abdominal exploration; and parathyroidectomy procedures that involve mediastinal or cervical exploration, sometimes requiring a sternal split or transthoracic approach. retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that encompasses a variety of operative methods across multiple organ systems. Specific procedures classified as methods of Excision include adrenalectomy (partial or complete) with or without adrenal gland biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches; arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from joints; cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration and choledochoenterostomy; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with ductal exploration, drainage, calculus removal, and optional transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region; nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct; and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, including variants that involve mediastinal exploration, sternal split, or transthoracic approaches. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 933
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of the thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically associated with the procedure استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). Thyroidectomy, whether performed as a partial or complete operation (thyroidectomy; partial or complete), is a targeted procedure for the head and neck structure and serves as the parent procedure for both استئصال الدرقية (full thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). The partial thyroidectomy (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي) frequently co‑occurs with دراق, reflecting a contextual relationship. Understanding these interconnections is essential for planning appropriate surgical management of thyroid cancer within the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2549
retrieved_docs.content: The term استئصال الدرقية is the Arabic name for thyroidectomy, a surgical procedure that involves removing all or part of the thyroid gland; it may be performed as a partial or a complete thyroidectomy depending on the underlying thyroid condition being treated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: The community defines Exploration as a broad surgical action within the Clinical actions A–G hierarchy that encompasses organ‑specific investigative and therapeutic operations across endocrine, biliary, breast/genital, neurosurgical, ophthalmic and spinal domains. In the endocrine system Exploration includes adrenalectomy (partial or total) with or without biopsy and possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors, as well as parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration that may be extended to mediastinal exploration via cervical, sternal‑split or transthoracic approaches. Biliary Exploration comprises cholecystectomy variants with common‑duct exploration—cholecystectomy with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy, sphincteroplasty—and choledochotomy or choledochostomy procedures that may involve drainage, calculus removal and adjunctive cholangiography; minimally invasive laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration is also included.
